coredata.dc:title: Water flow-driven N-CQDS/MoS2 piezoelectric-photocatalytic percarbonate degradation of oxytetracycline: Response surface modeling and mechanistic analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Li
Xing, Yuhao
Liu, Chenxu
Wang, Zhe
Jiang, Yi
Song, Tiehong
coredata.dc:description: To overcome the limitations of high energy consumption and low selectivity in conventional antibiotic treatment methods, this study developed a sodium percarbonate (SPC)-activated piezoelectric-photocatalytic system using a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/molybdenum disulfide (N-CQDs/MoS₂) composite to degrade oxytetracycline (OTC). The catalyst was synthesized via a hydrothermal method and characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM), and electrochemical analysis. Results showed that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots significantly enhanced the piezoelectric and photocatalytic performance of MoS₂. Reaction conditions were optimized using response surface methodology (RSM), determining the optimal parameters as OTC concentration of 10.68 mg/L, catalyst dosage of 0.46 g/L, and SPC dosage of 0.21 g/L. Under these conditions, a degradation efficiency of 99.52 % was achieved within 25 min, closely matching the experimental result of 99.18 %. The degradation kinetics followed a quasi-first-order model (k = 0.0752 min⁻¹), confirming the model’s reliability. Radical scavenging experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis identified h⁺, •OH, and O₂•⁻ as the main reactive species. Ecotoxicity assessment showed low toxicity of the degradation products, indicating good environmental safety. The catalyst maintained a high degradation efficiency of 96 % after five cycles, demonstrating excellent stability and reusability.

head.ce:title: Water flow-driven N-CQDS/MoS2 piezoelectric-photocatalytic percarbonate degradation of oxytetracycline: Response surface modeling and mechanistic analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome the limitations of high energy consumption and low selectivity in conventional antibiotic treatment methods, this study developed a sodium percarbonate (SPC)-activated piezoelectric-photocatalytic system using a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/molybdenum disulfide (N-CQDs/MoS₂) composite to degrade oxytetracycline (OTC). The catalyst was synthesized via a hydrothermal method and characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM), and electrochemical analysis. Results showed that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots significantly enhanced the piezoelectric and photocatalytic performance of MoS₂. Reaction conditions were optimized using response surface methodology (RSM), determining the optimal parameters as OTC concentration of 10.68 mg/L, catalyst dosage of 0.46 g/L, and SPC dosage of 0.21 g/L. Under these conditions, a degradation efficiency of 99.52 % was achieved within 25 min, closely matching the experimental result of 99.18 %. The degradation kinetics followed a quasi-first-order model (k = 0.0752 min⁻¹), confirming the model’s reliability. Radical scavenging experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis identified h⁺, •OH, and O₂•⁻ as the main reactive species. Ecotoxicity assessment showed low toxicity of the degradation products, indicating good environmental safety. The catalyst maintained a high degradation efficiency of 96 % after five cycles, demonstrating excellent stability and reusability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is classified as a new pollutant because of its high toxicity and resistance to degradation in the natural environment. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are widely used in treating antibiotics for their ease of operation, low energy consumption, and efficient removal of antibiotics. Commonly used AOP oxidants include persulfate [41], ozone [39], hydrogen peroxide [11], and percarbonate. Among these, percarbonates are widely used for antibiotic degradation due to their mild reaction conditions, broad pH range, cost-effectiveness, and relatively strong oxidizing ability [50]. Percarbonates can be activated by energy, metal ions, or alkalis [21,48], generating strongly oxidizing radicals (•OH, CO3 •-). However, these traditional activation methods are hindered by high energy consumption, long reaction times, and the risk of secondary pollution. The photocatalytic percarbonate system is an efficient and environmentally friendly method for degrading organic pollutants [47]. However, its application is limited by the intermittent supply of solar energy, variations in weather, and rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [27]. In the photopiezoelectric co-catalysis process, spontaneous polarization occurs when the piezoelectric material is subjected to external stress or strain. This promotes effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and facilitates rapid carrier migration to the surface of the piezoelectric material via the built-in electric field. The photopiezoelectric synergistic catalytic system effectively utilizes natural energy sources, including solar, wind, and wave energy [42], significantly improving energy efficiency and demonstrating its great potential for environmental remediation.

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) is a prominent piezoelectric-photocatalytic material, drawing significant attention in environmental catalysis due to its narrow bandgap, high surface electron concentration, ferroelectric properties, and excellent tolerance to structural deformation [44]. Li et al. [23] developed a defect-rich MoS₂-based piezocatalyst, showing high degradation efficiency of rhodamine B (RhB) under ultrasonic vibration via peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Similarly, Liu et al. [28] used multilayered MoS₂ nanoflowers as a piezocatalyst and applied ultrasound as a mechanical energy source to activate PMS for phenol degradation. While ultrasonic excitation can effectively activate the piezoelectric properties of MoS₂, its high energy consumption and equipment complexity hinder its feasibility for large-scale or practical engineering applications [20,24]. In contrast, water flow-driven systems have emerged as a promising alternative due to their eco-friendliness and low energy demands [45]. However, the low energy density of water flow limits its ability to fully activate the piezoelectric behavior of MoS₂. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), inexpensive and easily accessible nanomaterials with unique photophysical properties, are widely used in photocatalytic systems. However, their contribution to enhancing charge separation efficiency and improving piezoelectric performance remains limited. Karaca et al. [18] found that nitrogen doping effectively promoted charge carrier separation and induced asymmetry in the crystal structure of CQDs, enhancing their intrinsic piezoelectricity. Therefore, integrating nitrogen-doped CQDs into MoS₂-based piezoelectric-photocatalytic systems holds great potential to synergistically enhance catalytic performance and expand their application in green energy-driven pollutant degradation. Although significant progress has been made in recent years in using piezocatalysis for the degradation of organic pollutants, current research primarily focused on dye degradation. For example, Yan et al. [45] deposited hydrothermally synthesized CQDs onto g-C₃N₄ nanosheets via a co-precipitation calcination method. By combining the piezoelectric properties of g-C₃N₄ with the excellent charge transfer capabilities of CQDs, the piezocatalytic performance of the CQDs/g-C₃N₄ nanocomposite was significantly enhanced. Within 30 min, a degradation rate of 92.5 % for methylene blue was achieved. In contrast, studies on the efficient degradation mechanisms of oxytetracycline (OTC) driven by green mechanical energy remain relatively limited. Although water flow is a green and sustainable source of mechanical energy with considerable application potential, its effectiveness in enhancing the piezoelectric-photocatalytic efficiency of MoS₂ remains insufficient. Therefore, a key challenge in the field of environmental remediation lies in developing advanced catalytic systems that simultaneously offer efficient charge separation, enhanced piezoelectric response, and broad-spectrum visible light absorption, enabling the effective degradation of antibiotic pollutants.

This paper demonstrates the excellent performance of the water flow-driven piezoelectric-photocatalytic N-CQDs/MoS₂ activated percarbonate (SPC) system for OTC degradation, verified through a three-factor, three-level response surface methodology (RSM) experiment. The experimental results show that the hygromycin degradation rate reaches 99.52 % within 25 min. The reaction mechanism of N-CQDs/MoS₂ was further explored to confirm the dominant roles of h⁺, •OH, O₂ •⁻, ¹O₂, and CO₃ •⁻ in the degradation process and analyze the degradation pathways. Finally, the ecotoxicological evaluation confirmed the necessity of this study, highlighting its research value and practical application in environmental protection and pollution control.

All chemical reagents used in this study were analytical grade (AR, ≥99 %). Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, CAS 77–92–9), urea (CH₄N₂O, CAS 57–13–6), oxytetracycline (C₂₂H₂₄N₂O₉, CAS 79–57–2), ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH₄)₆Mo₇O₂₄·4H₂O, CAS 12054–85–2), thioacetamide (C₂H₅NS, CAS 62–55–5), and sodium percarbonate (Na₂CO₃·1.5H₂O₂, CAS 15630–89–4) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, C₁₀H₁₄N₂Na₂O₈, CAS 6381–92–6), p-benzoquinone (C₆H₄O₂, CAS 106–51–4), tert-butanol (C₄H₁₀O, CAS 75–65–0), phenol (C₆H₆O, CAS 108–95–2), and furfural (C₅H₄O₂, CAS 98–01–1) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

First, 3 g of citric acid was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 40 min. Then, 1 g of urea was added to the citric acid solution and stirred for 60 min until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was stirred, centrifuged, and the supernatant was collected. It was then dialyzed using a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off dialysis bag for 24 h, with the dialysis water replaced every 8 h. The dialyzed solution was subsequently freeze-dried under vacuum for 24 h. A brown-green powder was obtained, identified as nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MoS2 and N-CQDs/ MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs/MoS₂ was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method ( Fig. 1). First, 0.88 g of ammonium molybdate was dissolved in 50 mL of ammonia solution and sonicated for 40 min. Then, 0.94 g of thioacetamide was added and stirred for 30 min. Next, 10 mL of N-CQDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was added and stirred for 30 min to obtain a uniform mixture. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 13 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged, washed, and vacuum-dried at 70 °C to obtain a black MoS₂/N-CQDs powder. MoS₂ powder was synthesized using the same procedure without the addition of N-CQDs solution.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Bruker AXS D2 diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with a high-intensity monochromatic Cu-Kα radiation source (λ =0.15406 nm). The specific surface area and porosity of the samples were determined by nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 Plus instrument. The BET method was applied to calculate the specific surface area. The surface morphology and microstructure of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI Talos F200, USA), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer (USA). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the catalysts were obtained using a UV-3600 Plus spectrophotometer. Optical properties were further analyzed via photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer) and electrochemical measurements using a CHI-760E electrochemical workstation. Piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM) was performed using an MFP-3D Origin+ system (Oxford Instruments) to evaluate the piezoelectric performance of the samples.

To assess the catalyst's efficiency in activating sodium percarbonate (SPC) under a photo-piezoelectric synergistic system, a series of degradation experiments were conducted using oxytetracycline (OTC) as the model pollutant. A 300 W xenon lamp was used as the light source in the photo-piezoelectric catalytic experiments, positioned 10 cm above the reaction solution. Mechanical energy was applied via a magnetic stirrer to enhance the synergy between piezoelectric and photocatalytic processes, thereby promoting effective OTC degradation. Specifically, 25 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of OTC solution (10 mg/L) and stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, 10 mg of SPC was added to initiate the catalytic reaction. At 5-minute intervals, 1 mL of the reaction mixture was withdrawn and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove catalyst particles. The residual OTC concentration at each time point was analyzed using an Agilent high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system. The mobile phase was composed of acetonitrile and 0.1 % aqueous formic acid (15:85, v/v), with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. Detection was performed at 355 nm, and degradation was monitored by absorbance. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate, and the average value was reported to ensure data reproducibility and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BBD design and RSM experimental optimization process
ce:section.ce:para:
BBDs a factorial design that requires a minimum of three factors. It employs an incomplete block design, keeping one factor at the center point while varying the others through four combinations of their upper and lower levels. Based on BBD experimental results, response surface methodology (RSM) was used to predict and optimize the degradation efficiency of OTC. RSM enables solving multivariate equations and assessing the relative importance of influencing variables. The photodegradation rate was modeled using a second-order polynomial equation. The ranges and levels of the selected independent variables are listed in Table 1. The independent factors in this design—OTC concentration, catalyst, and oxidizer—were coded as A, B, and C, respectively. A total of 17 experiments were designed using (Eq. (1)):
(1)
N =
k
2
+ k + cp
where cp, k, and N represent the number of center point replicates, the number of factors, and the total number of experiments, respectively.

The output response (Y) of the Box-Behnken design (BBD) was defined as the degradation of OTC (%), modeled using a second-order polynomial equation with input parameters: X1 (OTC concentration), X2 (catalyst dosage), and X3 (oxidizer dosage). Eq. (2) was used to optimize the degradation process.
(2)

In this study, the variable “k” denotes the total number of independent variables, while the regression coefficients are represented by a₀, αᵢ, αᵢᵢ, and αᵢⱼ. Xi and Xj represent the independent factors, Y denotes the response of OTC degradation, and ε is the model error. To identify the optimal values for each variable, inverse response surface analysis and multivariate analysis of variance (ANOVA) were employed. Inverse response surface regression was applied to interpret the experimental data and estimate the values of the model terms. ANOVA was employed to evaluate the accuracy of the constructed model. The accuracy of the polynomial model was verified using the probability value (p-value) within a 95 % confidence interval. A p-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. A regression coefficient (R²) greater than 0.95 indicates that the developed model is accurate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of N-CQDs/MoS₂ composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of N-CQDs, MoS₂, and N-CQDs/MoS₂ samples. The broad diffraction peak observed at approximately 2θ = 23° for N-CQDs corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting an amorphous or low-crystalline structure. All diffraction peaks of MoS₂ match the hexagonal phase (JCPDS No. 37–1492), consistent with prior reports [32]. The XRD pattern of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite exhibits diffraction features similar to those of pristine MoS₂, indicating that the introduction of N-CQDs does not affect the crystalline structure of MoS₂. However, a slight decrease in the intensity of the (002) peak is observed, which can be attributed to the intercalation or surface coupling of N-CQDs in the MoS₂ layers. These results confirm the successful synthesis of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite material.

Fig. 3 presents the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite. The isotherm displays a typical Type IV curve in the relative pressure (P/P₀) range of 0.8–1.0, featuring a pronounced H3-type hysteresis loop. The average pore diameter is 49.811 nm, confirming the presence of a mesoporous structure. The BET surface area is 1.2461 m²/g, and the total pore volume is 0.016790 cm³ /g, indicating that the composite possesses sufficient porosity to facilitate reactant diffusion and enhance interfacial catalytic activity.

Fig. 4a–b depicts the flower-like morphology of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite, characterized by distinctive wrinkled and rippled structures. This morphology enhances the exposure of more edge-active sites, which can promote piezoelectric polarization [51]. Additionally, the elemental composition of the composite was visualized through elemental mapping, as shown in Fig. 4c–h. The EDS spectrum confirms the presence and distribution of C (27.3 %), N (1.2 %), S (40.9 %), and Mo (30.6 %) in the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite. No impurity peaks were detected, confirming that the N-CQDs were successfully loaded onto the MoS₂ surface.

Detailed analysis was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 5a–b show a non-aggregated lamellar structure of MoS₂, which increases the catalyst's specific surface area and thereby enhances its catalytic performance. Distinct lattice fringes of MoS₂ are observed in Fig. 5c, with spacings of 0.273 nm and 0.250 nm corresponding to the (100) and (102) planes, respectively, indicating a highly ordered crystal structure. Fig. 5d–e confirm that the lamellar structure of MoS₂ is preserved, suggesting that the introduction of N-CQDs does not disrupt its original layered structure or distribution. Fig. 5f shows that N-CQDs are uniformly distributed on the MoS₂ surface, maintaining a lattice fringe spacing of 0.250 nm, consistent with the (102) plane of MoS₂. These results are consistent with previous findings [13,29].

Fig. 6a shows the full XPS spectrum of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite, displaying signals for C, O, Mo, S, and N, confirming the successful loading of N-CQDs onto MoS₂. Fig. 6b shows the high-resolution C1s spectrum, with characteristic peaks at 284.1, 285.8, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–O–C, and O–CO bonds in the glycol units and carbonyl structures of N-CQDs [26]. Fig. 6c shows the high-resolution Mo3d spectrum with peaks at 227.9 and 231.3 eV, corresponding to the Mo 3d₅/₂ and Mo 3d₃/₂ orbitals, respectively [40]. Fig. 6d displays the high-resolution S2p spectrum with distinct peaks at 160.9 eV and 162.1 eV, assigned to the S2p₃/₂ and S2p₁/₂ orbitals of the Mo–S bonds in MoS₂. These findings confirm the successful coupling of N-CQDs with MoS₂, consistent with previous reports [25].

Fig. 7a illustrates that the N-CQDs/MoS₂ catalyst exhibits excellent photoresponse characteristics in both the ultraviolet and visible light regions. A strong absorption edge is observed around 400 nm, indicating the material's ability to efficiently harvest solar energy for driving photocatalytic reactions. The absorption band in the UV–vis DRS spectrum shows a steep edge, suggesting that the strong light absorption and electronic excitation behavior result from bandgap transitions within a specific wavelength range [36]. These transitions typically occur within a relatively narrow energy range. The optical bandgap (Eg) of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite was calculated using the Kubelka–Munk function as follows(Eq. (3)):
(3) (αhv)n=A(hv-Eg)

In this equation, α denotes the absorption coefficient, h is Planck’s constant, ν is the frequency of light, A is a proportionality constant, and Eg represents the bandgap energy. The exponent n depends on the nature of the semiconductor transition: n = 2 for direct bandgap semiconductors and n = 1/2 for indirect ones. As N-CQDs/MoS₂ is a direct bandgap semiconductor, n = 2 was applied in this study. As shown in Fig. 7b, the Tauc plot of (αhv)²－hv was used to estimate the bandgap of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ sample, which was found to be 1.66 eV [25]. Fig. 7c displays the valence band (VB) XPS spectrum, indicating that the valence band edge of N-CQDs/MoS₂ is located at -1.25 eV. Based on the equation ECB=EVB+Eg, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions were calculated as 0.41 eV and -1.25 eV, respectively. These results are consistent with previously reported values [34].

Generally, faster charge separation enhances the activation of peroxymonosulfate (SPC) [37]. The I-t curves of MoS₂ and N-CQDs/MoS₂ were compared using transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) ( Fig. 8a). The results show that, under light irradiation, N-CQDs/MoS₂ exhibits a stronger photocurrent response than MoS₂, indicating higher photogenerated carrier mobility. The EIS Nyquist plot in Fig. 8b shows that, compared to MoS₂, N-CQDs/MoS₂ presents a smaller arc, indicating lower charge transfer resistance and more efficient electron-hole separation, which favors the effective transport of photogenerated carriers. To further verify the photocatalyst’s photocarrier separation efficiency, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was employed. Lower PL intensity typically indicates higher photogenerated electron-hole pair separation efficiency and improved photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 8c, the PL emission intensity of N-CQDs/MoS₂ significantly decreases compared to pure MoS₂. The weakened PL signal of N-CQDs/MoS₂ suggests that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is effectively suppressed [16]. This is consistent with the findings from the tetracycline degradation experiments by [43].

The piezoelectric properties of MoS2 and N-CQDs/MoS2 materials were confirmed through PFM tests. In Fig. 9d, the bright regions of N-CQDs/MoS2 are significantly more abundant than those of MoS2 in Fig. 9a. Fig. 9e shows a higher electro-mechanical coupling effect compared to the region in Fig. 9b. Fig. 9f displays clear phase reversal regions compared to Fig. 9c, suggesting the presence of regions with opposite polarization directions, indicating typical piezoelectric domain structures. Fig. 10c shows a more typical “butterfly curve” than Fig. 10a, with the maximum response signal increased by two orders of magnitude. Fig. 10d shows a complete “zigzag” hysteresis loop compared to Fig. 10b, with the phase flip approaching 180°. In conclusion, N-CQDs/MoS2 exhibit superior piezoelectric properties, consistent with the findings of [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of influencing factors on piezo-photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: To highlight the catalytic performance of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite in degrading OTC, this study compared the degradation efficiencies of various systems. As shown in Fig. 11, the Vis/SPC system achieved a 45 % removal rate within 30 min, with a rate constant of k = 0.017 min⁻¹ . The Vis/MoS₂/SPC system reached a 57 % removal rate within 30 min, with k = 0.024 min⁻¹ . Notably, the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC system achieved 99.18 % removal within 25 min, with a rate constant of k = 0.075 min⁻¹ —4.41 and 3.13 times higher than those of the SPC and MoS₂/SPC systems, respectively. This enhancement is attributed to the N-CQDs modification, which promotes rapid transfer of photo-induced charge carriers and enhances the piezoelectric properties of MoS₂, thereby improving OTC degradation [1]. In summary, the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC system exhibits superior efficiency in OTC degradation.

In the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC system, irradiation time significantly affects the degradation efficiency of OTC. Degradation efficiency steadily increases with prolonged irradiation, following a typical stepwise growth pattern. During the early reaction stage (0–10 min), efficiency rose from 29.5 % to 46.0 %, indicating the initial generation of reactive species initiating pollutant degradation. At 15 min, degradation efficiency reached 63.5 %, suggesting active sites became saturated and the reaction rate accelerated. Efficiency peaked at 99.18 % at 25 min, indicating near-complete degradation within a short time. A slight decrease to 99.10 % at 30 min may result from the reaction nearing completion, fewer reactive species, or side reactions [31]. These results suggest optimal degradation occurs within 25 min, removing the need for prolonged irradiation and confirming the system’s rapid reaction rate and high efficiency. In summary, the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC system achieves efficient degradation rapidly and exhibits excellent photocatalytic responsiveness, demonstrating great potential for practical applications.

Fig. 12a illustrates the degradation efficiency of OTC (10.68 mg/L) using the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite under different light intensities (100 W, 300 W, 400 W, and 500 W). The results show that as the light intensity increases from 100 W to 300 W, the degradation rate significantly accelerates. However, when the intensity is further increased to 400 W and 500 W, the improvement in degradation efficiency plateaus or even slightly declines.This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that a moderate increase in light intensity enhances the photon flux, generating more photogenerated charge carriers and promoting the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as •OH and •O₂⁻, thereby improving pollutant degradation efficiency. Nevertheless, once the active sites on the catalyst surface become saturated, further increases in light intensity offer limited enhancement to the reaction rate [35]. Therefore, a 300 W light source was selected as the optimal illumination condition for subsequent experiments, balancing both photocatalytic efficiency and economic feasibility. Fig. 12b compares the degradation performance under different light sources, including xenon lamp, mercury lamp, blue light, sunlight, white light, red light, and darkness. As shown, degradation is more effective under xenon lamp, mercury lamp, and blue light, but significantly weaker under red light and in the dark. This indicates that the composite has a good visible light response and strong absorption in the blue light region. Some degradation also occurs in the dark, possibly due to the material’s piezoelectric effect or partial reactions triggered by the oxidative nature of SPC [2]. In summary, both the light source and irradiation intensity significantly influence the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/MoS₂. Xenon lamp irradiation is particularly effective in generating and separating photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby accelerating pollutant degradation.

The Box-Behnken design (BBD) was used to further validate the influencing factors and their interactions. Table 2 presents the predicted and experimental values of OTC degradation efficiency obtained from the Box-Behnken matrix after 17 experiments. A second-order polynomial equation (Eq. (4)) was derived by analyzing the response variable (degradation efficiency %) in relation to the three independent components.
(4) Y(%)= +99.42-1.63A-1.98B+ 1.35C-2.00AB-5.25AC-3.05BC-4.36A2-2.56B2-4.81C2
where Y represents the degradation efficiency, and A, B, and C are coded as −1, 0, and 1, respectively.

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted to identify the main factors affecting the photodegradation efficiency of OTC, as shown in Table 3. A factor was considered statistically significant if the absolute value of t exceeded 2 or if the p-value was below 0.05. Model terms were deemed significant if their p-values were less than 0.05. Hence, a lower p-value and a larger t-test value indicate a more significant factor [9,30]. The F-value of the quadratic model is 185.54, with a p-value of less than 0.0001, indicating that the model is highly statistically significant and that the selected factors have a substantial effect on the response variable. The single factors A, B, and C, as well as their interaction terms AB, AC, and BC, were significant (P < 0.01), indicating their important role in the photodegradation of OTC. The quadratic terms A², B², and C² were also significant (P < 0.01), suggesting a clear quadratic effect in the system and further confirming the nonlinear characteristics of the response surface. The residual analysis revealed a small error (1.92), and the P-value of the misfit term was 0.1193 (P > 0.05), indicating that the model was well-fitted and there was no significant misfit. The model's coefficient of determination, R² = 0.9958, adjusted R² = 0.9892, and predicted R² = 0.9430, indicates a very high degree of fit and predictive ability. Adeq Precision = 36.6032, which is significantly greater than 4, suggests that the model is suitable for optimizing experimental conditions [15]. Fig. 13a was used to verify whether the residuals followed a normal distribution. The data points are approximately aligned along the diagonal line, indicating that the residuals follow a normal distribution with no significant deviations or outliers. The underlying assumptions of the RSM are satisfied. Fig. 13b compares the experimental response with the predicted response, indicating a better fit between the expected and actual degradation efficiency of OTC.

As shown in Fig. 14a, as the catalyst dosage increases, the self-masking effect is triggered, reducing the degradation efficiency [22]. The degradation curve of OTC first increases and then decreases, indicating the presence of an optimal catalyst dosage. When the catalyst amount is constant, degradation efficiency decreases with increasing pollutant concentration due to excessive pollutant molecules occupying the active sites on the catalyst surface, thereby limiting its catalytic effectiveness [23]. Fig. 14b illustrates the effect of OTC concentration and oxidant addition on degradation efficiency. As the oxidant concentration increases, the degradation efficiency initially increases and then gradually levels off, suggesting that excessive oxidant may cause a free radical self-burst phenomenon, reducing degradation efficiency [33]. Similarly, degradation efficiency decreases with increasing pollutant concentration [10]. As shown in Fig. 14c, a certain amount of catalyst and oxidizer in the N-CQDs/MoS2/SPC system significantly increased OTC degradation efficiency. However, degradation efficiency may decrease with high catalyst and oxidant concentrations, possibly due to excessive oxidant triggering unwanted side reactions or catalyst particle agglomeration, which reduces its activity [49].

Based on the density of contour lines and the magnitude of surface changes, the factors affecting OTC degradation efficiency were ranked as follows: pollutant concentration > catalyst dosage > oxidizer dosage. The optimal conditions for hygromycin degradation by N-CQDs/MoS2 were as follows: an initial pollutant concentration of 10.68 mg/L, catalyst dosage of 0.46 g/L, and oxidant concentration of 0.21 g/L, resulting in 99.52 % degradation efficiency within 30 min ( Fig. 15). This is consistent with the experimental result of 99.18 % degradation efficiency and a quasi-primary kinetic coefficient of 0.0752 min−1 ( Fig. 16). The reliability of the optimized conditions for maximum degradation efficiency was confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radical quenching experiments and mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: TBA, EDTA-2Na, B-pQ, FFA, and PhOH were employed as scavengers for •OH, h⁺, O₂•⁻, ¹O₂, and CO₃•⁻, respectively, to investigate the primary reaction mechanisms in the degradation of OTC by N-CQDs/MoS₂. As shown in Fig. 17a, the addition of EDTA-2Na, B-pQ, and FFA significantly inhibited OTC degradation, suggesting that h⁺, O₂•⁻, and ¹O₂ play major roles in the process. The inhibitory effect followed the order: h⁺ > O₂•⁻ > ¹O₂. In contrast, TBA and PhOH caused only weak inhibition, indicating that •OH and CO₃•⁻ have a limited role in this system. These findings are consistent with those reported by Yanmei et al. [46]. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments confirmed the presence of O₂•⁻, ¹O₂, and •OH in the system. As shown in Fig. 17b, the addition of DMPO resulted in four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, confirming the presence of •OH radicals. In Fig. 17c, four peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 were detected after DMPO addition, confirming the formation of O₂•⁻ radicals. In Fig. 17d, TEMP was used as a singlet oxygen (¹O₂) trapping agent. Under light irradiation, a distinct triplet signal with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 was observed, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of TEMP-¹O₂ and confirming ¹O₂ generation during the reaction.

Based on the experimental results, we propose a plausible reaction mechanism shown in Fig. 18 and supported by Eqs. (5)–(13) . Under weak visible light and fluid flow, the internal electric field in the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite drives photogenerated electrons and holes to migrate in opposite directions, causing charge carriers to accumulate on the catalyst surface (Eq. 5). Photogenerated holes (h⁺) directly oxytetracycline OTC molecules. Meanwhile, electrons (e⁻) proceed via two pathways: (1) reacting with dissolved oxygen to produce superoxide radicals (O₂•⁻) (Eq. 6); and (2) activating hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), formed by sodium percarbonate (SPC) decomposition, to yield hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (Eqs. 7–8). Subsequently, O₂•⁻ and •OH react to form highly reactive singlet oxygen (¹O₂) (Eqs. 9–11). This singlet oxygen then reacts with carbonate ions (CO₃²⁻) to produce carbonate radicals (•CO₃⁻) (Eq. 12). These reactive species collectively drive the multi-radical synergistic degradation of OTC (Eq. 13). In summary, incorporating N-CQDs enhances the composite’s light-harvesting ability and improves MoS₂’s piezoelectric properties by modulating its structural symmetry. Fluid-induced mechanical disturbance causes deformation of MoS₂ nanosheets, generating an internal piezoelectric field. This field effectively promotes separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, prolongs carrier lifetime, and enhances reactive oxygen species generation. These synergistic effects highlight the strong potential of this catalytic system for mineralizing and remediating organic pollutants.

The intermediates were identified using high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS). Based on previous research [17], it was inferred that OTC degradation followed a pathway involving dehydration, deamination, deamidation, dehydroxylation, and demethylation. As shown in Fig. 19 , OTC initially underwent dehydration to form the intermediate OTC1 (m/z = 443.14). OTC1, after losing its N-methyl group, was converted to OTC7 (m/z = 429.13), which then underwent a ring-opening reaction to yield OTC8 (m/z = 419.14). In an alternative degradation pathway, OTC underwent dehydration, dehydroxylation, and deamidation reactions to form OTC2 (m/z = 383.14), which was further degraded to OTC4 (m/z = 372.10) through hydroxylation and loss of N-methyl groups. OTC4 was further decomposed via cleavage to form OTC5 (m/z = 279.12), which was then dehydroxylated to yield OTC6 (m/z = 263.12). In the final stage, OTC6 and OTC8 were further decomposed into smaller molecular weight compounds: OTC9 (m/z = 104.01), OTC10 (m/z = 165.09), OTC11 (m/z = 114.10), and OTC12 (m/z = 179.10), ultimately leading to complete mineralization into CO2 and H2O.

This study assessed the acute and chronic toxic effects of 12 OTC degradation intermediates on fish, Daphnia, and green algae in aquatic ecosystems through ecotoxicological analysis. As shown in Fig. 20a-c, the logarithmic distribution of acute toxicity (LC₅₀/EC₅₀) revealed that all OTC degradation intermediates exhibited lower ecotoxicity than OTC itself, with most falling within the harmless region. Fig. 20d-f, which analyze the chronic toxicity risk (Chv), indicated that OTC3, OTC9, and OTC12 were within the harmless region, while OTC2 exhibited a higher chronic toxicity risk, though still lower than that of OTC itself. In summary, the overall toxicity of OTC after photocatalytic degradation was lower, indicating that this catalytic system not only efficiently degrades OTC but also ensures good ecological safety.

To systematically evaluate the stability of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite, this study assessed its performance through three aspects: cyclic degradation efficiency, structural changes characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), and nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms. Fig. 21a shows the results of five consecutive cycles of oxytetracycline (OTC) degradation catalyzed by the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite. With a fixed reaction time of 30 min per cycle, the degradation efficiency reached 99.18 % in the first run and remained above 96 % in the fifth, indicating excellent catalytic performance even after repeated use. These results suggest that the incorporation of N-CQDs not only enhances the catalytic activity of MoS₂, but also improves its structural stability and cycling durability [4]. Fig. 21b displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite before and after the catalytic reaction. The major diffraction peaks remained largely unchanged, and the characteristic peaks of MoS₂ were still clearly visible, indicating that the catalyst’s crystal structure was well preserved during the reaction. This further confirms the structural stability of the composite [6]. Fig. 21c–d show the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite before and after the catalytic reaction, along with corresponding BET surface area and pore structure parameters. Before the reaction, the material exhibited a BET surface area of 1.2461 m²/g, a pore volume of 0.016790 cm³ /g, and an average pore diameter of 49.811 nm. After the reaction, the BET surface area slightly decreased to 0.9802 m²/g, the pore volume dropped to 0.012027 cm³ /g, and the average pore diameter declined to 45.420 nm. The overall reduction was minimal, indicating that the composite maintained a stable porous structure with sufficient diffusion pathways and active sites [3]. In conclusion, the N-CQDs/MoS₂ composite catalyst exhibited strong structural integrity, high catalytic efficiency, and good surface area retention after multiple cycles, underscoring its excellent stability and reusability potential.

To confirm that N-CQDs/MoS₂ functions as a typical heterogeneous catalyst, a hot filtration test was conducted. The procedure involved mixing a 10.68 mg/L oxytetracycline (OTC) solution, 0.46 g/L N-CQDs/MoS₂ catalyst, and 0.21 g/L sodium percarbonate (SPC), followed by 15 min of visible light irradiation. Immediately afterward, the mixture was hot-filtered at room temperature through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the solid catalyst. The filtrate was then irradiated under the same conditions for an additional 10 min (until 25 min total), and samples were collected to measure residual OTC concentration. Results showed a degradation efficiency of 64 % at 15 min. After catalyst removal, continuing the reaction for 10 more minutes under the same conditions increased degradation by only 5 %. This confirms the catalyst’s dominant role and verifies the heterogeneous nature of the reaction system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of photocatalytic AOPs for pollutant degradation performance
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC composite system developed in this study for degrading the antibiotic oxytetracycline (OTC), Table 4 compares the degradation efficiencies of various photocatalytic systems across different pollutants. Results show that under low catalyst loading (0.46 g/L) and SPC dosage (0.21 g/L), our system achieved 99.18 % degradation within just 25 min, significantly outperforming most conventional systems. For instance, while the Vis/Cu/ZSM5/SPC system reaches similar degradation efficiency, it requires 40 min, and the UV/α-Fe₂O₃/SPC system takes up to 90 min. These findings indicate that our system exhibits faster reaction kinetics and higher catalytic activity.

Moreover, it operates under near-neutral pH conditions, effectively avoiding corrosion risks and secondary pollution commonly associated with acidic systems, thereby offering superior environmental adaptability and application potential. Notably, the introduction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) markedly enhances the piezoelectric response and photocatalytic activity of MoS₂ by promoting the generation of key reactive species (h⁺, •OH, and O₂•⁻). This highlights the innovative advantage of piezo-photocatalytic synergistic activation of percarbonate.In conclusion, the Vis/N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC composite system demonstrates outstanding performance in terms of degradation efficiency, reaction speed, environmental compatibility, and operational simplicity, providing a promising strategy for efficient and eco-friendly antibiotic wastewater treatment.

This study successfully prepared N-CQDs/MoS₂ piezoelectric-photocatalytic composites, and their structure and properties were verified by XRD, SEM, EDS, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The introduction of N-CQDs not only improved the piezoelectric performance of MoS₂ but also promoted electron transfer to MoS₂, enhancing the activation efficiency of percarbonate (SPC) and thereby improving the degradation of oxytetracycline (OTC). Response Surface Methodology (RSM) combined with the Box-Behnken Design (BBD) was used to systematically optimize the key factors affecting the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of OTC. Experimental results showed that under optimal conditions (initial pollutant concentration of 10.68 mg/L, catalyst dosage of 0.46 g/L, and oxidant concentration of 0.21 g/L), a degradation efficiency of up to 99.52 % was achieved within 30 min. The actual experimental degradation efficiency reached 99.18 %, with a quasi-first-order kinetic coefficient of 0.072 min⁻¹ . The experimental values were consistent with the predictions of the quadratic polynomial regression model. Active species trapping experiments identified the roles of h⁺, •OH, O₂•⁻, ¹O₂, and CO₃•⁻ in the degradation process. Based on the radical scavenging experiments and HPLC-MS analysis, degradation pathways of OTC in the N-CQDs/MoS₂/SPC system were proposed, including dehydration, deamination, and deamidation. Recycling experiments demonstrated good catalyst stability, maintaining 96 % degradation efficiency after five cycles. In summary, this study not only advances the application of piezoelectric-photocatalytic technology in environmental remediation but also provides a theoretical basis and practical guidance for the development of new, efficient photocatalytic materials.

Yi Jiang: Validation. Zhe Wang: Project administration, Methodology. Tiehong Song: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Li Sun: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Chenxu Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Yuhao Xing: Software, Formal analysis.
